@RickyPo @C0PE My recommendation: Deposit it in an OA repository. Then share URL to the repo copy, not the journal copy. If you like, add an explanation to a cover page before depositing. Few if any legit journals would pub the paper under these circumstances, but repo deposit would be easy.

@RickyPo @C0PE For completeness: I always recommend that repo copies (of postprints) include a full citation and link to the published edition, and recommend the same here. If the author thinks the journal is an embarrassment, that's a good reason to include an explanation as well.

1/ Kudos to @DouglasTodd for exposing the wrongful treatment of Derek Pyne, an economist suspended without pay by @ThompsonRiversU for his campaign against scam journals. 
https://t.co/2JPlfJqG0m

2/ Unfortunately Todd made an elementary side mistake. He wrote, "The rise of rogue journals has in part come out of well-meaning new requirements by governments in both Canada and the U.S. that any research funded by taxpayers should be published in #openaccess journals."

3/ Untrue. These policies require deposit in OA repositories, not publication in OA journals. (In the jargon, they're green mandates, not gold mandates.) They're compatible with publishing in conventional, non-OA or paywalled journals.

4/ Please fix that and carry on. The error feeds the widespread and harmful misunderstandings that all OA is gold OA, and that all OA mandates are gold mandates. The truth on this point is consistent every other important point in the article.

A victory for Dems, #Maine, *and* #RankedChoiceVoting (RCV). The loser even went to court to overturn RCV, which had the side-effect of solidifying it. Now time for RCV to expand to states beyond Maine. 
https://t.co/9faM9Cbe8I

RCV eliminates the spoiler effect. For example, in 2000, Dems could have voted for both Al Gore and Ralph Nader, if they wished, and ranked them in either order. Nader votes would not subtract from Gore votes, and Gore would have beaten GWBush in a landslide.

Example from the right: In 1996, Republicans could have voted for both Bob Dole and Ross Perot, if they wished, and ranked them in either order. Perot votes would not subtract from Dole votes, and Dole would have beaten Clinton in a landslide.

@jbrittholbrook @AmericanPublish Journals that use open licenses in order accommodate authors funded by the Plan S coalition members (or by NIH, or ...) are "granting" permission. They were free to refuse the work. This is bargaining, or balancing of pros/cons, not coercion.

@jbrittholbrook @AmericanPublish Ditto. Plan S coalition money comes with a string attached, like all other money. Open licensing is a condition on a voluntary contract. Authors needn't apply for grants with that condition. Whatever the outcome, it's a decision, balancing pros/cons, not coercion.

OATP is a #crowdsourced service running on #freesoftware to capture news & comment on #openaccess to research. It publishes real-time alerts (in 8 file formats) to help stay on top of what's happening, & organizes knowledge of the field by tag for easy searching & sharing.

1/ The new version of #Plan_S was released today.
* Announcement 
https://t.co/dMU2uNxajy
* Text of the new principles and implementation guidance
https://t.co/puAQIYtSo7
2/ One highlight important to me: The repository-based (green) compliance option is more explicit and less onerous.

3/ This frees coalition grantees to publish in non-compliant journals. This is good for early-career researchers (#ECRs) under pressure from promotion & tenure committees to favor journals unlikely to become plan-compliant.

4/ Yes, we want those committees to improve their practices, and coalition support for @DORAssessment already takes steps in that direction. But that cultural change will move slowly. Meantime, widening the green option frees ECRs to satisfy funder and committee at the same time.

5/ Non-compliant journals wanting to publish coalition-funded research must allow authors to take the green option. Must let them retain the rights needed to deposit their articles in repositories and make them OA immediately under a CC-BY license.

Kudos to @WellcomeTrust for strengthening its already-strong #openaccess policy to match the new version of #Plan_S: Zeroing out embargoes, requiring rights retention, increasing support for @DORAssessment, and halting APCs to hybrid journals. 
https://t.co/iyf1WYhIan

1/ @Elsevier says that choosing the #openaccess option in its hybrid journals will comply with the new version of #Plan_S. 
https://t.co/55tlCLM5iz
2/ That's not true, unless Elsevier is signalling that its hybrid journals stand under transformative agreements and plan to convert to full (non-hybrid) OA on a definite timetable.

3/ Plan S, Principle 8: "The Funders do not support the ‘hybrid’ model [except] as a transitional pathway towards full Open Access within a clearly defined timeframe, and only as part of transformative arrangements."

I've been reading about scholarly journals for years, inc their history, but never ran across the following fascinating pattern noticed by Derek de Solla Price. See his _Science Since Babylon_, enlarged edition, Yale University Press, 1975, Chapter 8 ("Diseases of Science").

From pp. 163-164: "The earliest surviving journal is the Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society of London, first published in 1665. It was followed rapidly by some three or four similar journals published by other national academies in Europe."

"Thereafter, as the need increased, so did the number of journals, reaching a total of about one hundred by the beginning of the nineteenth century, one thousand by the middle, and some ten thousand by 1900."
"According to the World List of Scientific Periodicals, a tome larger than any family Bible, we are now well on the way to the next milestone of a hundred thousand such journals."
From p. 169: "The most remarkable conclusion obtained from the data just considered is that the number of journals has grown exponentially rather than linearly."

"Instead of there being just so many new periodicals per year, the number has doubled every so many years. The constant involved is actually about fifteen years for a doubling, corresponding to a power of ten in fifty years and a factor of one thousand in a century and a half."
"In the three hundred years which separate us from the mid-seventeenth century, this represents a factor of one million."
Let's call this Price's Law, by analogy with Moore's Law.

I'm glad to say that there's an authorized #openaccess edition of Price's book.
https://t.co/eKEe5ChjMI
But because it lacks page numbers, I'm using page numbers from the print edition.

Just learned that there's already a Price's Law (aka Price's square root law), named after the same Derek Price: "Half of the publications come from the square root of all contributors."
https://t.co/eR5lOBr2Hm
We could call the new one Price's journal-doubling law.

(I'm sorry the article is paywalled. It needs a wider audience. Author @willyfrederick raises and rejects the money answer.)

1/ Has anyone proposed this before? When someone pays an article processing charge (#APC) for an #openaccess article, they are *investing* in the publication. If the publisher makes a profit from the work, the investor should get a cut.
2/ If the APC covers 100% of the production costs, including overhead, then the investor cut could (but needn't) approach 100% of the profit.
3/ Note that many APCs pay *more* than 100% of the production costs. They pay production costs plus whatever the pub thinks the market will bear. Part of the APC itself is part of the pub's profit.
4/ The same idea could (but needn't) apply to book processing charges, not just article processing charges.
5/ Some OA publishers are nonprofit. The same idea could (but needn't) apply to the surpluses of nonprofit pubs, not just the profits of for-profit pubs.

6/ For now, I'm not proposing this idea. I'm looking to see whether anyone else has proposed it. If someone has, I haven't seen it and welcome pointers. I'd like to read the discussion it might have triggered.
7/ Or if this is a new idea, I'm proposing it in order to trigger some discussion. I won't be ready to endorse it without working through the discussion.
8/ Here are some complexities I readily grant (tweets 9-14 below). To me, they're not reasons to dismiss the idea. They're reasons to start or read the discussion.
9/ Granted: it will be hard to determine how much profit a particular article brings to a journal publisher, or how much profit a particular book brings to a book publisher.

10/ Granted: it will be hard to determine what the investor cut ought to be, as a percentage of the pub's profit/surplus, esp if we want the pub to have *some* profit/surplus in order to grow the enterprise.

11/ Granted: even if we've decided the % question above, it will be hard to determine the investor cut, as an absolute $ figure, let alone collect it, if pubs don't share their business data. (But note that #Plan_S will require that kind of transparency.)

12/ Granted: most APC payors today are nonprofit orgs (universities and funders). If they take the investor cut, it would have to be reframed or redistributed. It was profit to the pub but can't be profit to a university or foundation.
13/ Granted: it will be hard to predict whether APCs would go up or down. Paying out investor cuts could be a reason for pubs to raise or lower their APCs.

14/ If this idea worked, it could (but needn't) stimulate a new class of APC investors. They could subsidize OA publication while lifting the APC burden from univs, funders, and authors. Granted: That has at least two upsides. But what are some other likely consequences?
15/ Discussion questions: Are these problem worth solving? If so, what are the best solutions?

More thinking out loud. Taking an investors cut is compatible with CC-BY and the way it allows others to sell and even profit from the work. But explaining how (esp how demanding one is compatible with demanding the other) will not be short or easy.

1/ John Conyers died last week. He was a major progressive on civil rights and a major reactionary on #openaccess. He was also accused of sexual harassment by two women.
https://t.co/0UieMNcrfV
2/ In this thread I'll focus on his #OA record and ignore the rest, without intending to discount it.
3/ He was the chair of the House Judiciary Committee, which handled all copyright-related legislation. That should have made him an expert on copyright.

4/ But he regularly repeated publisher falsehoods on the copyright position of the NIH policy and FRPAA (Federal Research Public Access Act). FRPAA was an early pro-OA bill, and the predecessor of FASTR, the Fair Access to Science and Technology Research Act.

5/ When publishers lobbied to repeal the NIH policy, and prohibit other US agencies from adopting similar policies, they lied about the policy and copyright law to support their position. Conyers introduced their bill and spoke their lines.

6/ Because this anti-OA bill was popularly called the Conyers Bill (full title = Fair Copyright Research Works Act), its tag in the Open Access Tracking Project is "oa.conyers".
https://t.co/Ys99a5AzgT

7/ The year Conyers introduced the bill (2008), he received more money from Elsevier than any other member of the House.
https://t.co/0KLqF5MEfA

1/ OK, I'm a boomer (born 1951), and I'm tired of seeing thin-skinned and forgetful fellow boomers chastise millennials and gen-xers for saying "OK boomer."

Here are four quick reminders for my peers:
2/ First, we boomers have been known to dismiss our elders. It was one of our specializations.

3/ From our friend Bob: "Mothers and fathers throughout the land, don’t criticize what you can't understand. Your sons and your daughters are beyond your command. Your old road is rapidly agin'. Please get out of the new one if you can't lend your hand." (OK, he said "please.")
4/ Second, millennials and gen-xers have been the targets of unjust boomer criticism -- for laziness, prolonged adolescence, naïve idealism. That's what our parents and grandparents said about us.

5/ Boomers who don't remember this are senile. (Yes, went there.) Boomers who remember it but think it was unfair for our parents, and fair for us, are unwilling to turn the tables. That's lazy and adolescent.
6/ We didn't have a snappy term like "boomers" for our parents and grandparents. But I remember using the similar retort, "OK gramps."
7/ If "OK boomer" is ageist, then we were ageist in our day, in exactly the same way, and proud of it.

8/ Third, it's not as if we boomers are hip to all things millennials and gen-xers are hip to. We're not. Even when we're happy about the difference, we can't forget that it's the same kind of difference that provoked us to dismiss our elders.
9/ Fourth, sticks and stones. Really. To my peers: If you pretend to be the grown up in this squabble, then behave like one.

10/ [Footnote. I'm not saying that all boomers, when younger, criticized, dismissed, or eye-rolled their elders. It's enough to say it was common. But if I had to guess, I'd say it was even more common in our generation than it is today.]

11/ Meant to add this 5th reminder but forgot. Maybe the most important. Our generation is leaving our successors a catastrophically screwed up world. My peers: You have to admit that even if you spent your life working for a better one.
@agrudjr Yes, so we were even *more* ageist! Another reason to ease up on "OK boomer" users.

@DorothyBeach Agree that we didn't mean to make anything worse, & many tried to make things better. I count myself in that group. But if we look at the world, & not our intentions, can't fault the millennial rebuke. Yes, boomers need to do *far* more on #climate, not leave it all to next gen.

1/ As of today, here's the breakdown of deposits in DASH, the @harvard #openaccess repository:
* 40,371 (79.6%) journal articles
* 5,657 (11.1%) ETDs
* 806 (0.2%) conference presentations
* 3,858 (0.8%) other

2/ Most institutional repositories don't publicly display their genre breakdowns. Hence, we can't tell whether this is a typical spread. 

(We don't yet display our breakdown either bc this feature isn't built in to the software. But we want to, and have it on our to-do list.)

3/ Note that our contents lean heavily toward peer-reviewed journal articles. That's deliberate. Articles and ETDs are at the top of our priority list. Our faculty-adopted OA policies focus on articles.

4/ Note the reverse leaning at #Elsevier-owned Digital Commons repositories. "Across more than 500 Digital Commons repositories, we estimate that 94% of the content & 91% of the downloads are for materials *other* than...peer-reviewed journal articles."
https://t.co/xTBlcaNF1N
@matt_ruen We welcome grey lit in DASH and share it whenever we can. Same with other categories such as books and book chapters.

https://t.co/5bck86ypVN

I quote some excerpts below if you don't want to click through.
6/ This is no [ordinary] market. "Scholarly publishing is permeated by state action, public subsidies, gift culture, and anticompetitive practices."

7/ "Scholarly journals...benefit from public subsidies. Most...research is funded by...public money, conducted & written up by researchers working at public institutions & paid w/ public money, & then peer-reviewed by faculty at public institutions & paid with public money."

8/ "Even when researchers and peer reviewers work at private universities, their institutions are subsidized by publicly funded tax exemptions and tax-deductible donations. Most toll-access journal subscriptions are purchased by public institutions and paid with taxpayer money."
9/ "Last and not least, publishers exercise their control over research articles through copyright, a temporary government-created monopoly."

Here are some snippets. 
#COVID19

2/ "We had no such thing as printed newspapers in those days [1664] to spread rumours....[We had only] letters...and...word of mouth...so that things did not spread instantly over the whole nation, as they do now."

3/ "But it seems that the Government had a true account of it, and several councils were held about ways to prevent its coming over; but all was kept very private. Hence it was that this rumour died off again, and...we hoped [it] was not true."

4/ "The calamity was spread by infection....I cannot but with some wonder find some people...talk of its being an immediate stroke from Heaven, without the agency of means,...which I look upon with contempt as the effect of manifest ignorance and enthusiasm."

5/ "Some endeavours were used to suppress the printing of such books as terrified the people, and to frighten the dispersers of them,...but nothing was done in it,...the Government being unwilling to exasperate the people, who were, as I may say, all out of their wits already."

6/ "If all the infected persons were effectually shut in, no sound person could have been infected by them....But...the infection was propagated insensibly, and by such persons as were not visibly infected, who neither knew whom they infected or who they were infected by."

1/ A well-known experiment showed that people in a room with disgusting smells answered moral questions on a questionnaire more harshly than controls in a clean room answering the same questions.
https://t.co/1SFAXEN9Vh
2/ The experiment triggered debates that continue to this day. But however you interpret the underlying phenomenon, think about how it affects remote learning.

3/ Think about how hard we try to keep classrooms clean, quiet, uncluttered, even austere. Think about our students self-isolating in environments that differ vastly from one another and from our unnaturally clean, tidy, and quiet classrooms.

4/ We can't (easily) change our students' home environments. Or even (easily) know how widely they vary. Or even (easily) take that variation into account when teaching. We can shift to pass/fail grading. But can we take into account how environments affect cognition?

5/ BTW, I'm not saying that classrooms are the best environments for learning, though I see the case for them. I'm wondering how skilled & sensitive teachers might take into account that their students are learning differently now, diff from before & diff from one another.

In this new blog post, I summarize the results of seven studies and welcome suggestions for any that I missed.
2/ Thanks to @dannykay68, @andy_nobes, and @osiunited for recent Twitter discussions and interactions that pushed me to pull this together.

3/ Twitter-sized summary:  In the global north, APCs are usually paid by the author's employer or funder, not by the author out of pocket. In the south, most APCs, or a plurality of them, are paid by authors out of pocket (depending on the study).

I'm a white male who writes academic nonfiction.
2/ I've written four academic books and co-edited one academic anthology. I got an advance of $2k for one of the monographs (Open Access, MIT Press, 2012), and zero for each of the others.

1/ John Stuart Mill has a good answer to people who only tolerate protest & dissent from those who are calm & civil (his term is "temperate").

The passage is too long for Twitter, so I've posted it to my blog.
https://t.co/XjPCIqjkYT

Here I paraphrase some of his key points:

2/ The rule to stay civil might be welcome if imposed on all alike. But it's usually imposed only on protesters, & not those representing "prevailing opinion". Establishment figures are often applauded for incivility, vituperation, & righteous indignation against protesters.
3/ If we adopt the rule, then protesters & establishment figures should be judged with equal harshness for anger & incivility. But they are not. Protesters are judged more harshly.

4/ If we adopt the rule, then we need to agree on what counts as incivility, and this seems hopeless. Establishment figures tend to regard any "telling and powerful" argument against them as offensive and uncivil.

5/ Vituperation from the powerful tends to silence the powerless more than the other way around. Hence, in the interest of "truth and justice", it would be better to deter vituperation from establishment figures than from protesters.

1/ This is big. #Plan_S has incorporated rights retention into its OA strategy. 
https://t.co/CaZyi1UaL1

2/ This makes the green OA compliance option much easier for grantees. It allows grantees submit coalition-funded research to journals that don't meet the gold OA compliance conditions. It enhances author freedom to submit to the journals of their choice.

3/ #ECRs no longer face the painful choice between their promotion/tenure committee and their funder. They can now satisfy both. If need be, they can publish in the kind of journal expected by the P&T committee and use green OA to comply with the funder OA policy.

As of today, the @DOAJplus lists 15,049 peer-reviewed #openaccess journals. Of those only 4,108 (27.3%) charge APCs. 10,941 (72.7%) charge no APCs.

I just looked this up for a colleague and thought I'd pass it along.

Here's how to look this up yourself in the future. I wish it were easier. 

* Go to DOAJ <https://t.co/i3uy3t7br6>.
* From the front page, note the total number of journals (upper right corner). 
* Then click on Search (next to Home in the top navigation bar).

* Then click on + Journals vs. Articles. 
* Then click on Journals. 
* Then click on + Article processing charges (APCs). 
* You'll see "No", "Yes", and "No info", each followed by a number. 
* Use the total journal tally and these numbers to compute the percentages.

1/ I'm old enough to remember when you could only send email to people who used the same email provider you did. 

Has anyone seen a good account of how we transitioned from that system of siloed rivals to the email system we have today?

2/ I'm interested in learning the lessons of that transition and trying to apply them elsewhere. You could call it the triumph of interoperability over network effects.

As you can see, this "triumph" has sputtered and failed in many other domains. Why did it work in email?

@jacintodavila That looks like part of the answer, but probably not all, for this reason: Where interop has *not* yet triumphed, we often have the standards needed to ground its triumph. And where we don't, we could be working on them.
@GavinMoodie Yes, good point. Now I want an even larger history, one covering both the phone system and the email system, and perhaps others that failed to make the same transition.

1/ #Originalism is not the only approach to the interpretation of written law. Interested in the respected alternatives? 

[Selfie alert] Let me recommend _The Case of the Speluncean Explorers: Nine New Opinions_.
https://t.co/z7T9tncK09

2/ Lon Fuller wrote the first five Supreme Court opinions on this case in 1949, to illustrate the prevailing and divergent judicial philosophies of his day. I wrote nine new opinions in 1999, to illustrate how much, and how little, had changed in the intervening half century.

3/ Fuller invented a fascinating fictitious case for his judges to decide, letting him show how these different judicial philosophies worked in practice. Because the case is so riveting, it even makes the reading fun.

David: Apologies for using your tweet as the springboard for a thread of responses. I keep trying to kill this publisher arg (that pubs need exclusive rights to protect against unauthorized use) and you've give me one more chance. https://t.co/Bvdqe1DlMA

2/ "It's inaccurate and disingenuous to argue that publishers need exclusive rights to prosecute plagiarists. First, the rights are rarely used this way. Plagiarism is typically punished by the plagiarist's institution, not by courts --that is, by social norms, not by law."
3/ "Second, if it's ever desirable to pursue a plagiarist in court and authors don't give publishers the right to do so on their own, then authors retain that right to use as they see fit."

4/ "Third, many authors would rather have a larger audience and impact [through #openaccess] than give their publisher the seldom-used legal tools to prosecute plagiarists. Authors should make this decision, not publishers."

5/ "Finally, if an author discovers a plagiarist & the pub really wants to get involved, the author can always delegate the pub to act as his/her agent. For this purpose, pubs don't need rights from the time of publication, nor do they need exclusive rights."

6/ "Behind this [pub] argument there's a confusion of plagiarism & © infringement. Someone can commit plagiarism w/o infringing © (by copying a fair-use excerpt & claiming it as one's own) & infringe © w/o committing plagiarism (by copying a larger excerpt but with attribution)."
7/ "One can also commit both together (by copying a large excerpt and claiming it as one's own), but that doesn't collapse the distinction. One can commit just about any two offenses together."

1/ Analogy to climate change:

Individuals can reduce their carbon footprint & they should.

Individuals can advance #openaccess by making their own work OA & they should (https://t.co/eERB6SvdIK). 

But institutions can do far more than individuals.

https://t.co/4edJbjFdfW

2/ Part of what individuals can do is persuade institutions to act, change course, or strengthen what they are already doing. 

Think about what universities, societies, publishers, funders, and governments could do, and what you could do to nudge them along.
3/ It's misleading and harmful to blame slow OA progress on slow individuals, at least if that lifts responsibility from slow institutions.

4/ Many institutional leaders are willing to act but reluctant to get out ahead of their members and constituents. They need to feel bottom-up pressure. They need political cover. They need internal support.

1/ I'd put this historically. "Gold OA" originally meant OA delivered by journals regardless of the journal's business model. Both fee-based and no-fee OA journals were gold, as opposed to "green OA", which meant OA delivered by repositories. https://t.co/fBZISTtHGM

2/ Over time, some referred to fee-based gold as "gold" without qualification. That was sloppy, like referring to complex carbs as "carbs" without qualification. Sometimes we need adjectives to resolve ambiguity.

3/ Complex carbs are carbs. But "carbs" (without a qualifying adjective) doesn't unambiguously denote complex carbs. Sloppiness would become error if "carbs" came to mean complex carbs alone & exclude simple carbs. This is what happened to "gold OA". Sloppiness became error.

4/ As this sloppiness and error became more common, the meaning of "gold OA" drifted. Many who didn't know the original meaning thought "gold" positively excluded no-fee OA journals, contradicting the original concept.

5/ I accept that meanings change as usage changes, even when usage changes because of sloppiness or misunderstanding. (I'm a descriptivist.) I don't insist that "silly" must still means "blessed" just because it once did.

6/ I understand why some people are confused. We're in the middle of a period of drift. The drift of "silly" is over. But (for example) we're still living through the drift of "enormity" to include "enormousness", which causes confusion.

7/ I'm torn. I fought sloppiness and error about "gold" in the name of precision. However, I know that at some point it's useless (and silly) to deny the reality of drift. But have we reached that point?

8/ To me, it's still worth fighting the drift of "literally" to include "figuratively". It's new enough to be sloppy and wrong. But we should accept the drift of "silly" to exclude "blessed" because usage has settled. However, the drift of "gold OA" is neither so new nor so old.
9/ I have no problem using the term "diamond" for no-fee OA. "Diamond" never meant both fee-based and no-fee. But "gold" did once mean both, and to many people still does.

10/ The term "open access" underwent similar drift. It originally meant access free of both price & permission barriers. But early on many used it to mean access free of price barriers alone. I introduced the gratis/libre terms in 2008 to enable precision.
https://t.co/B2oWYOYruc

11/ In the case of "open access" we generally succeeded in reversing the regrettable drift (which in 2008 I thought was unwinnable). Today most people realize OA means removing both price and permission barriers. Can we reverse the drift of "gold OA"?

12/ If you do want to push back against the drift: (1) Use "gold OA" to mean OA delivered by journals regardless of the j's business model. (2) Don't call APC-based OA "gold" w/o qualification. Call them "fee-based or "APC-based" OA. Or "fee-based" or "APC-based" gold.

13/ For the record, there are good reasons to avoid the term "gold" for general audiences, apart from drift & ambiguity. It's not self-explanatory; it's positively opaque. Same for "green", "diamond", "platinum", "bronze". They're insider jargon, like the tech terms in any field.
@mchris4duke Correct + thanks. Just as gold OA can be fee-based or no-fee, green OA can be embargoed or unembargoed.
@BinWalters @mchris4duke Yes, I mentioned them. But I made clear that embargoes are not part of the definition of green.

1/ Here's a "machine-generated literature review".
https://t.co/EqXI53DW4E

As I read the announcement, "human-machine collaboration" might be a better description.

2/ It's outside my field & I have no opinion on its merits. I'd love to hear from experts in the field ("Climate, Planetary & Evolutionary Sciences") who do have opinions.

But I do have some assumptions, at least about pure-AI literature reviews…

3/ Which will we see first? 
(a) AI-generated literature reviews as good as the average human-generated literature reviews?
(b) AI-generated literature reviews that cover the topic comprehensively and summarize the contributions accurately?

4/ I say the (a), on the ground that most human reviews don't live up to the standard in (b), and AI will match human capabilities before it exceeds them. 

But even reviews meeting the standard in (a) will be huge boon.

5/ I like the idea of AI-generated lit reviews. It's a job suited to AI & hard for humans to do *well*. Failures in comprehensiveness & accuracy can be subtle, hard to avoid, & hard to detect. Today & probably for a long to come it will also be a job hard for AI to do *well*.
6/ For obvious reasons, well-done AI-generated literature reviews will cover #openaccess literature before they cover a mixed corpus of OA and non-OA literature.

@judell @SpringerNature I'd start with Guido Visconti (the human editor/collaborator in this case) or Stephanie Preuss (the Springer-Nature voice for this project). Sorry I don't have contact info for either one.

1/ If AI peer reviewing tools are trained on real submissions and their real fates, then (to put it crudely) they will share the biases of current reviewers. In particular, they'll share biases against authors writing in a second language. 
https://t.co/EpPOggbsQQ
2/ Journals using these tools for gatekeeping could perpetuate bias, even if analyzing the data "could potentially expose [these] biases."

3/ One suggestion in the paper: Let authors use these tools to improve manuscripts before submission. "This might be of particular benefit to authors for whom English is not a first language…whose work…may be likely to be adversely affected by first impression bias."
4/ Me taking this a step further: These tools will only improve — at matching human judgments (biases and all), reducing bias, or flagging possible problems for others to judge.

5/ In each of these cases, two groups will benefit at least as much as journals:
* authors (to make papers more acceptable to journals, if that's what they want)
* readers (to make journals less necessary)

Mixed feelings about open peer review. Yes to all the benefits cited by others. But anonymity has benefits too.
1. It protects women, minority, & young/unknown authors from referee bias.

1/ Academic publishers don’t need exclusive rights. They don’t need them *for publishing*. They don’t need them to make a work public or add value in the form of peer review, metadata, copy editing, formatting &c. Nor do they need them to make enough $$ to pay their bills & grow.

2/ Publishers only need exclusive rights for monopoly control over the published work & any revenue it might yield. Publishers who say they need exclusive rights are saying they need this monopoly control.
3/ The best evidence that publishers don’t need exclusive rights is that so many do without them, for example, #openaccess publishers using CC-BY.

4/ Publishers who drop demands for exclusive rights make author rights-retention an automatic side effect of the publishing contract. They make author rights-retention as easy as possible to understand and implement. Authors should favor publishers who do this.
5/ Publishers who do this will probably boost their submissions. It will attract some authors and deter none. No authors will shun a journal just because the journal shuns exclusive rights.

6/  I'm ready to write up this thesis at greater length but want to share the gist of it this way first. The longer version would mainly add some fine print about the right of first publication & the belief that monopoly control is sometimes the only way to make enough money.

@JPSchimming 1/ Good question. Here's my answer from 2007, which is still my position today.
https://t.co/DNZLQCIkiN

@JPSchimming 2/ "It's inaccurate and disingenuous to argue that publishers need exclusive rights to prosecute plagiarists. First, the rights are rarely used this way.  Plagiarism is typically punished by the plagiarist's institution, not by courts —that is, by social norms, not by law."

@JPSchimming 3/ "Second, if it's ever desirable to pursue a plagiarist in court and authors don't give publishers the right to do so on their own, then authors retain that right to use as they see fit."

@JPSchimming 4/ "Third, many authors would rather have a larger audience and impact than give their publisher the seldom-used legal tools to prosecute plagiarists. Authors should make this decision, not publishers."
@JPSchimming 5/ "Finally, if an author discovers a plagiarist and the publisher really wants to get involved, the author can always delegate the publisher to act as his/her agent."
@JPSchimming 6/ "For this purpose, publishers don't need rights from the time of publication, nor do they need exclusive rights, let alone a policy to limit access to the author's work."

1/ From @libErinOwens: "On average, scholarly communications librarians experience impostor phenomenon more frequently and intensely than academic librarians more broadly."

This is an important. I have some thoughts in the thread below.

2/ This point matches my experience: "The specialty changes rapidly as new technologies and metrics emerge, copyright laws & interpretations evolve, & researcher & publisher behaviors change; on top of that, the field addresses many questions without clearly defined answers."

3/ I can add that the field changes so fast that staying on top of what's happening is ≈ impossible. I know because I try. I also run a crowd-sourced project (@oatp) to stay on top of what's happening & I'm very conscious that we need a bigger crowd.

4/ I can add that library schools tend not to teach scholcomm at all, teach it badly, or stint on it, making it just a module in a larger course. Most scholcomm librarians have to learn the job on the job.
5/ I can add that scholcomm jobs are relatively new. There's a very foreshortened 'tradition' against which to compare your skills, strengths, experience, and general competence.

6/ I can add that many libraries put the whole load of scholcomm work on one person rather than an office. There's too much for one person to do. The more you know the field, the more you recognize your inability to cover the territory. Same is true for most scholcomm *offices*.

1/ I'm changing my role at @Harvard & @HarvardLibrary.

2/ I'm stepping down from my position as director of the Office for Scholarly Communication (@OSCHarvard). But I'll continue in a new half-time role devoted to internal & external consulting on #openaccess, starting next summer.

3/ This is a shift, not retirement. Actually it's two shifts — from directing/managing to consulting/advising & from full-time to half-time. Both are at my request. I asked for this new arrangement & thank Harvard Library for agreeing to it.
4/ I'll continue my affiliation with the Berkman Klein Center for Internet & Society (@BKCHarvard).
5/ I'm not ready to retire & not even close. But shifting to half-time & continuing to consult on OA is exactly what I want right now.
6/ I have a new book to finish, new grant ideas, & life is short. (Did I mention that I'm 70?)

7/ I say a bit more about my new role here, on a wiki page that I can keep more or less up to date. 
https://t.co/lAZcaxuDt0
8/ Harvard Library will also issue an announcement describing the changes to my role & to the Office for Scholarly Communication. I'll add the link to this thread when it comes out.

9/ The library will soon open the search for my successor. I'll also add the link to the ad when it comes out. Preview: The library is using the occasion rethink the position & restructure the office in ways that are all to the good.
10/ It has been an unmixed joy to work with dedicated & first-rate colleagues within the library & across the university…

11/ …esp those who worked in @OSCHarvard: Anne Britton, Kyle Courtney, Becky Cremona, Colleen Cressman, Amanda Cullin, Nick Dials, Tom Dodson, Reinhard Engels, Emily Kilcer, Sarah Kinne, Sue Kriegsman, Colin Lukens, Bill McKinney, Arlene Navarro, Stuart Shieber, & Ben Steinberg.
12/ If I say more now, this will feel more like a good-bye than a shift. Thank goodness it's not a good-bye.

1/ A friend just asked what was the most comprehensive list of no-fee (no-APC) #openaccess journals. I thought I'd share my answer here. 

It's the @DOAJplus. Here's how to carve out just the DOAJ subset of no-fee journals:

2/ Go to the DOAJ. https://t.co/Otl1Mee8qb
* Click on Search in the upper left corner. 
* In the drop-down menu, click on Journals.
* Scroll down a bit and click the checkbox, "Without article processing charges (APCs)."

3/ Now you'll see just the no-fee journals. From the same page you can add other search filters if you like.

Today DOAJ lists 12,002 no-fee OA journals, from a total of 17,211. As has been true for about 15 years, about 70% (69.7%) of peer-reviewed OA journals are no-fee.

4/ Although the DOAJ is the most comprehensive list, it's not complete. Some worthy journals haven't yet asked to be included. Some have asked & DOAJ is vetting them. And of course, DOAJ deliberately excludes some based on its vetting.

1/ Under the traditional #patent bargain, a company gets a limited monopoly in exchange for sharing its knowledge with the public. Something for the private interest & something for the public interest. Here's a Japanese idea striking the reverse bargain. 
https://t.co/oCMO8D2Ayf

2/ When the govt wants to keep a patentable discovery secret, the company gets no patent but does get the monetary equivalent, a payment for "roughly 20 years of licensing income." In exchange it needn't share its discovery with the public.

3/ I'm not endorsing this idea, just fascinated & thinking out loud. It's a completely different way to give something to the private interest & something to the public interest — dropping both the monopoly & the public knowledge.

The Budapest Open Access Initiative (@TheBOAI) just released its 20th anniversary recommendations. #BOAI20

* Announcement (English version)
https://t.co/vXJ4kIYcSF

* Recommendations themselves
https://t.co/k6Yc6pq4hj

From #BOAI20: "[#Openaccess] is not an end in itself, but a means to other ends, above all, to the equity, quality, usability, and sustainability of research. We must assess the growth of OA against the gains and losses for these further ends."

#BOAI20: "Host and publish OA texts, data, metadata, code, and other digital research outputs on open, community-controlled #infrastructure. Use infrastructure that minimizes the risk of future access restrictions or control by commercial organizations."

#BOAI20: "Reform research #assessment…to improve incentives. Adjust…assessment practices for funding decisions and university hiring, promotion, and tenure decisions. Eliminate disincentives for OA and create positive new incentives for OA."

#BOAI20: "Favor inclusive publishing and distribution channels that never exclude authors on economic grounds. Take full advantage of OA repositories and no-APC journals (“green” and “diamond” OA). Move away from article processing charges (#APCs)."

#BOAI20: "Favor models which benefit all regions of the world…controlled by academic-led…organizations… avoid concentrating new OA literature in commercially dominant journals & avoid entrenching models in conflict with these goals. Move away from read-and-publish agreements."

#BOAI20: "OA research is safest in academic-led institutions and infrastructure not subject to future acquisition, not subject to profit-maximizing access decisions, and not subject to the risk of enclosure."

#BOAI20: "Careful [research assessment] reforms can eliminate disincentives for OA and create new incentives for OA, without limiting the topics, conclusions, or rigor of research. They are entirely compatible with academic freedom and the highest standards of quality."

#BOAI20: "We recommend moving away from article processing charges (#APCs). Viable alternatives have long existed, but they are systematically under-noticed, under-discussed, under-appreciated, under-funded, and under-used."
#BOAI20: "Just as we seek to remove paywalls to enfranchise more readers, we seek to remove #APCs to enfranchise more authors."
#BOAI20: "When institutions support OA primarily by paying #APCs, they give no-APC or #diamond journals a perverse incentive to start charging APCs."

#BOAI20: "The task [in moving away from APCs is] to stop excluding authors on irrelevant grounds, stop distorting research through those exclusions, stop obscuring the economics of OA, stop paying more than necessary…" [cont. in next]

#BOAI20: [cont. from previous]: "…stop the flow of limited funds from the academic sector to the commercial sector, stop subordinating the sustainability of research to the sustainability publisher revenues, and stop entrenching a business model with these consequences."
#BOAI20: "Universities and funders paying significant amounts of money in #APCs should invest in #green and #diamond OA instead."

#BOAI20: "Read-and-publish agreements have the beneficial effect of increasing the number of OA articles. We welcome this growth but worry about making it a foreground goal pursued at the expense of background goals."

#BOAI20: "[APCs] don’t pay for improved quality, but for the perception of improved quality…Career advancement can depend on that perception. But that is a problem to solve, not an immutable reality to accommodate…"

#BOAI20: "…The most equitable, sustainable, and academically honest solution [to that problem] is for research assessment committees to pay less attention to where research is published and more attention to the quality of research itself."

#APCs at hybrid journals are still more expensive than APCs at full or non-hybrid #openaccess journals.
https://t.co/5k3QuwYA57

Here are some of my questions, concerns, and comments about Musk and Twitter.

* He wants to add an edit button. Good; me too.
https://t.co/wVGcU2nyRX

But for now that's all I can say on the plus side.

* He hates spam bots. Good. But what about useful bots, like @OATP?
https://t.co/Tojrgzie4D

Press coverage blurs the distinction between spam & non-spam bots. I'm sure today's T employees understand the difference. But does Musk himself? What about the next wave of T employees?
* He wants to open-source the algorithms. I ready to support that. But in this case, will access to the code allow well-funded bots to return after eviction, and punish just the ill-funded?
* He wants to make T more welcoming to people who are "censored" today. Who will tell him that dropping or weakening content moderation will make T less welcoming?
* T is already very reluctant to remove accounts and flag abusive content. To make it even more reluctant will only open the spigot to more hate, harassment, intimidation, and disinformation.
* Though many people call T a public square, many of us chose it precisely because it's not a public square and because can impose rules that would not work in a public square.

* @JoshuaPotash has put together a good collection of occasions when Musk opposed or punished free speech.
https://t.co/sK32oTXgUi

* Musk is thin-skinned. See his response to Elizabeth Warren for suggesting that he should pay his fair share of taxes. (He called her "Senator Karen".) The sole owner of a social-media platform is going to take some slings and arrows. Can he handle it?
* If harassment and disinformation increase, will advertisers leave? Will Musk care?
* Employees at Tesla and SpaceX have quit because of discrimination and sexual harassment. What will happen with T employees?

* Who will stay, who will go, and who will join? Many users I respect will leave. Many I respect will go. Many I disrespect will join. Conceivably many I respect will join, but I'm not predicting it.

Because I can mute and block, I care more about who stays than who joins.
* I'll stay long enough to see the trajectory, which includes seeing who else stays. I'll also use T alternatives and see how good they become.
* Will T alternatives become much better with the new influx of users? Better in people, features, moderation, and decentralization? They should and I'll be watching with interest.

* The fact that I have many friends, allies, and trusted news sources on Twitter won't necessarily stop me from leaving. The same fact didn't stop me from deciding that my office should quit Facebook.
https://t.co/53lJOUGp29

* @TomDNaughton asks a good question. "Anyone get the feeling Twitter engineers are frantically changing the algorithm before Musk makes it open source?"
https://t.co/SNx0bzwAQ0
* What happened to the board's poison pill? Did it repeal it after agreeing to Musk's offer?
* Does anyone care that Musk seems to have acquired his first 9.5% of the stock illegally? When he exceeded 5%, he was supposed to notify the @SECGov but didn't.

* His lack of disclosure kept stock prices low, helping his own purchase plans. But it hurt stockholders with plans to sell, and some are suing him for it.

Could this interfere with the acquisition itself?

* Will Musk try to make T more profitable? He doesn't have to and has even said, "I don’t care about the economics at all." If he wanted to, he could let it lose money every year for the rest of his life.

* But Musk is taking on significant debt to make this purchase. If he wants T profits to pay back that debt, T will have to make more revenue/year than it has been making.
https://t.co/D2hjqANct4
* Will Musk get bored? Distracted by his other businesses? He already has a lot on his plate.

* Musk made his offer on April 14. The EU adopted the Digital Services Act on April 23. The T board approved his offer on April 25. T will have to comply with DSA. But Musk almost certainly didn't take that into account. Is he fine with that? Will it cramp his plans?
* Apart from changing to reflect Musk's preferences and priorities, how will T change simply because it will be private?
* If T becomes the "free speech" enclave that Trump wanted Truth Social to be, will new T kill off Truth Social?

* Will the new T allow Trump to return? 

Trump says he doesn't want to return. But he utters falsehoods fluently, which is why he was kicked off.
https://t.co/khPdDIqbHJ
* The acquisition needs to be approved by shareholders and the SEC. What will they say? I believe the SEC can't block the acquisition, but could slow or modify it.

* Jack Dorsey not helping. He's said he doesn't believe "anyone should own or run Twitter." For sure. But he's also said that "Elon is the singular solution I trust." That's a contradiction. 

This is just woo: "I trust [Musk's] mission to extend the light of consciousness."

1/ This is big. @WHOSTP is calling on all federal research funding agencies to adopt #openaccess policies. If they already have OA policies, they must strengthen them to meet the new guidelines. I'll add some summary points in a thread below. https://t.co/CLA5zHwKTG

2/ Here's the new (8/25/22) @WHOSTP memorandum.
https://t.co/ZsbFxchbfh

It updates and strengthens a 2013 @WHOSTP memorandum from the Obama White House. For background, here's the 2013 memo.  
https://t.co/EXu0DZjsiH

3/ TLDR. Here are the four biggest changes:
* No more embargoes. New policies must require immediate or unembargoed OA. 
* New policies must cover data too.
* All fed funding agencies must now adopt policies, not just the largest ones. 
* The new policies are all green.

4/ The Obama memo only applied to the largest funding agencies (those funding $100m or more in extramural research). The @NEHgov was just a bit too small to make it. The Biden memo covers all fed funding agencies. So for the first time, fed OA policies will cover the humanities.

5/ The new policies must cover peer-reviewed publications and data. If they want, agencies can go further and cover "peer-reviewed book chapters, editorials, and peer-reviewed conference proceedings."

6/ The peer-reviewed publications must be deposited in "agency-designated" OA repositories and in "formats that allow for machine-readability and enabling broad accessibility through assistive devices."

7/ The no-embargo rule applies to data too, not just to texts. Data must be open at the time of publication. (Which data exactly? Data "underlying peer-reviewed publications resulting from federally funded research".)
8/ Agencies covered by the 2013 Obama memo have six months to submit draft updates to their existing OA policies. Agencies not already covered have one year.

9/ Agencies must "coordinate…to enhance efficiency & reduce redundancy in…plans and policies." This matters because US universities will have to comply with all of them. Policies may have to differ, to reflect disciplinary differences. But the less they differ, the better.

10/ The draft new agency policies will be reviewed by @WHOSTP and @OMBPress. Coordination among the agencies will be managed by the National Science and Technology Council Subcommittee on Open Science.

@WHOSTP @OMBPress 11/ Publishing in an OA journal will not count toward compliance. Covered works must be made green OA in the right way (designated repository, no embargo). This is 100% compatible with gold OA. But gold OA is not necessary and not sufficient.

12/ @SPARC_NA just issued its press release on the OSTP action.

@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens I see value in distinguishing "green-only" OA articles (OA thru repositories but not journals) and "green+gold" OA articles (OA thru both repos & journals, even if green is AAM and gold is VOR). Not wedded to the clunky terminology. But is that the same distinction?

@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens I favor open licenses for both gold and green. But I think the licensing terms of gold OA have nothing to do with the the definition of green. To me, green OA is OA delivered by repositories (regardless of version, license, embargo, and so on).

@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens The licensing terms of gold are worth discussing elsewhere, but not when defining green (or varieties of green). If a work is OA through a repository, it's green, even if another version with an arbitrary license is gold.

@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens To me "green-by-gold" is confusing because no article is made green by virtue of any aspect of a gold version. It's made green by deposit on an OA repository. Likewise, no article is made green by virtue of a paywall a journal version.
@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens I don't see it that way. To me, no definition of gold would affect whether an article is OA through a repository (my definition of green).

@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens This is getting too complex for Twitter. But an article is green if it's OA through a repository, regardless of where the permission came from to make it OA through a repository. Moreover, preprints are green even though there's no journal version yet.

@lisalibrarian @YvonneCampfens I agree that it's useful to tag the subset of green articles that happen to have gold versions (the subset I call green+gold). But green OA articles are green by virtue of their deposit in OA repositories, not by virtue of the license on the gold version.

3/ In this thread, I want to add two quick corrections or supplements to the article.

4/ The article says that our guide lists all US university OA policies. But the list is limited to policies "of the kind recommended in the guide" — a much smaller list. It also goes beyond the US to policies in Europe, Africa, Asia, & Australasia.
https://t.co/mFFcP70SLl

5/ At Harvard, librarians are not faculty, and the school-level OA policies cover faculty alone. Given the focus of the article, I want to add how we cover librarians. 

We did that in two steps:

6/ First, we exempted librarian scholarship from the work-for-hire doctrine. Second, we adopted a voluntary form giving non-faculty researchers (librarians, staff, students) the same rights-retention benefits that the school-level OA policies give faculty.
